verbatim_text: I made the observation at the time that there was a ton of C++ code around in the autopilot which was the software 1.0 code and then there was some neural nets in there doing image recognition and. I observed that over time as we made the autopilot better. Basically the neural network grew in capability and size and in addition to that all the C++ code was being deleted and like was and. A lot of the capabilities and functionality that was originally written in 1.0 was migrated to 2.0. So as an example, a lot of the stitching up of information across images from the different cameras and across time was done by a neural network and we were able to delete a lot of code and. So the software 2.0 stack quite literally ate through the software stack of the autopilot. So I thought this was really remarkable at the time. I think we're seeing the same thing again where. Basically we have a new software and it's eating through the stack. We have three completely different programming paradigms and I think if you're entering the industry it's a very good idea to be fluent in all of them because. They all have slight pros and cons and you may want to program some functionality in 1.0 or 2.0 or 3.0. Are you going to train neurallet? Are you going to just prompt an LLM? Should this be a piece of code that's explicit etc.
